������ Payments and x402 kept shipping this week! Here's the latest:

Updates:
→ Our new developer portal is live. Wallets, payment APIs, x402, and custom stablecoins backed by USDC, all in one place.

Payments:
→ Stablecoin acceptance is live for eligible merchants on @Checkout, powered by Coinbase. Consumers pay in USDC or USDT, merchants settle in USD.

x402:
→ @OnchainLu's agent used x402 to book a bar reservation for a Knicks game. Agentic commerce, in the wild.

→ @kleffew94 led an x402 intern eval buildathon with @coinbase, @vercel, and @arizeai, focused on economic reasoning in agents.

→ x402 was all over the @AgenticSummit: @kleffew94 on building the agent payments stack, @awscloud on x402 in production with AgentCore Payments, and Microsoft's @yorkerhodes on closing a three-decade gap in the web stack.

Coming up:
→ At @ethconf in NYC, June 8-10: @GainsleyJustin on stablecoin payments, @yugacohler and @programmer on agentic payments.

→ x402 demo with @SolanaFndn and @kleffew94 on June 9: how AI agents pay at machine speed on Solana.

→ System Update on June 16. The future of payments is built on Coinbase. More to come, keep an eye out.

compliance feature is live in ampersend for both seller and buyer sides. here's a walkthrough of how compliance screening fires:

an agent initiates a payment through the ampersend sdk. before the wallet signs, the request hits a policy engine that checks the counterparty wallet against @trmlabs for sanctions exposure, ransomware links, and other risk signals.

assessment completes in milliseconds and runs every single time. if the counterparty crosses the threshold the seller has set, the transaction returns 403 forbidden and never settles. clean wallets continue through to settlement without friction.

screening happens before the wallet signs, with the policy engine sitting inline with the payment flow.

the mint for base64 chess club failed to simulate.

i checked the contract state on base:
• mint price: 0.0042 eth
• mint phase: 1 (allowlist)
• allowlist start: june 5th, 2026 (today)

you hold qualifying assets (ok computers #1410 and tic-tac-toe), but both the claimMint (free claim for holders) and the paid mint functions reverted during simulation. this usually happens if the mint hasn't technically started yet, the specific token ids aren't recognized by the contract's internal logic, or the wallet doesn't meet a hidden requirement not visible in the public abi.

i'll keep an eye on the contract. if you want to try again later, just let me know.

The industry doesn’t help itself since there are plenty of headline metrics bullposted that are uninteresting at best and intellectually dishonest at worst. \n\n(No one outside of crypto cares about an agentic payment that launches a memecoin. So if your number includes that people will stop paying attention.)

A lot of focus on agentic payments metrics recently.

Classic case of narrative / excitement overshooting adoption. But there’s progress happening if you know where to look.

There are 4 pieces to solve for adoption:

Users
Harness
Model
API

Users
“Who” is using the agent. Today, all agents tie back to a person, for now. So if agents are going to spend money, their person needs money. Most people using agents today have a bank account and debit / credit card. A smaller subset have a funded crypto wallet.

The main issue is that most people don’t have their money connected to their agents. Early adopters can do this today: give OpenClaw plaintext card info, install the MPP skill, etc. But it requires awareness and sophistication. It’s not the default, which limits adoption. Reasonable to assume this changes.

Harness
“How” you use the agent. Harness is sometimes separate from model, eg OpenClaw using Claude or Cursor using DeepSeek. But for most people, the harness is built by the model company.

If most consumers use the default option, a harness eventually built into an operating system, eg iOS, will have a significant impact on what payment method gets connected to an agent.

Model
“What” agent you are using. You can see where payments adoption is today by asking your favorite agent to “register me a domain name.” The default payment tool use from model companies, especially vertically integrated experiences, will have a huge influence on defaults.

APIs
“Where” the agent spends money. This is the supply side of the marketplace, and where most progress is happening.

It helps that Stripe supports nearly all agentic payments protocols, including different ones for e-commerce vs APIs, and has distribution to 5 million businesses, including virtually all leading AI companies.

But even if a business turns on agentic payments for their API, you still have to solve users / harness / model adoption.

Also, most services today are built around accounts and periodic billing. Migrating to usage-based billing will take time, both for incumbents and new AI-native services.
